messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe the governance structure, decision-making processes, risk management, and quality assurance.

3.2 METHODOLOGY We have split the project in 9 WPs with a total duration of 36 months (Figure 6: A2C methodology) . The work is organised in three parallel, interrelated, blocks: Block 1 is focused on R&D activities, includes methodologies for EFSA authorisations, DIS development (WP1), organic agri-food wastes recycling by green extraction routes (WP2), multilayer plastic wastes sorting and recyling including pilot upscaling (WP3), upcycling of the recycled agrifood materials into new food, nutraceutic & cosmetic formulations & upscaling of the optimal extraction routes (WP4), upcycling of the recycled plastic materials into high added value susbtances for cosmetic, PHBV bioplastics and compounds for food packaging & agriculture, including pilot scale up (WP5). Block 2 is dedicated to demonstrate the A2C systemic solution adoption, replication and scalability. Entails the integration of the technologies at pilot scale, including a validation of the products in a industrial environment and the DIS available for the public as well as the development of CEBMs of the demonst rated tecnologies and its financing mechanisms (WP6). WP7 constructs, validates and demonstrates through 2 replica tion cases, by means of public e ngagment and cocreation processes, a model for A2C adoption & replication based on the multidimensional contextual study and addressing ideintified barriers while tools and recommendations to decision makers and stakeholders will be delivered. Block 3 is dedicated to horizontal activities : Dissemination, communication, exploitation, IPR, standarisation, training, policy recommendations (WP8) and project management (WP9). Block 1. Research and developme nt activities (WP1-WP5): Methodology based on control loops to adjust the processing conditions in order to achieve the optimal results WP1. A2C Specifications, Residues Management and Data Integration System will be l ed by CETEC, as project coordinator, and will be focus on new products specifications, guarantee of safety of the A2C products and processes under EU standards, energy & water efficiency assurance and implementation of the Data Integration System. WP2. A2C technologies for the recycling of th e organic agri-food wastes is dedicated to the implementation of optimal routes for the extraction & purification of phenolic compounds , carotenoids and fib res from the agri -food wastes and will be led by CTNC . WP3. A2C Figure 6.A2C project methodology 16 technologies for the sorting and recycling of multilayer plastics residues will include the optimal technologies for the recyling of the multilayers, and will be led by GWC ( plastics recycler expert). All WP3 echnologies will be pilot scale up based on lab trials, and by-products recovery strategies will be implemented. WP4. Upcycling of the recycled agrifood materials and upscaling of optimal extraction & purification routes is focused on demonstra te the applicability of the fibres, phenolic sybstances & carotenoids extracted in the development of new formulations for food, nutraceutics and cosmetics achieving the properties of the formulations based on conventional extracts. It will be led by DMC (expert in development of innovative food & nutraceutics solutions). WP5. A2C technologies for the upcycling of the recycled plastics materials is dedicated to obtain high added value products from the recycled plastic materials and will be led by UNIMIB (expert in niotransformation processes). Block 2. Activities for demonstration of A2C systemic solution adoption, replication and scalability (WP6-7) WP6. Demonstration of the A2C technological solution integrates all the developed technologies at pilot scale up technologies (GWC, SAPE, IRIS, BOKU. MELLI, CETBIO, UNIMIB, UA, WTS, CTNC, DMC, CITRO, ALM, MCT, MKSI, SSICA) in a demonstrator in the Murcia (Spain) at GWC, CETBIO, CETEC, DMC and CTNC facilities (all of them in close industrial areas) evaluating their performance in an industrial environment, and will be led by GWC (with a vast experience in process upscaling and whose facilities will include most of the pilots). Moreover, by - products valorisation strategies will be implemented ( CTNC, WTS, CEW, CTNC. CETBIO, UA, SAPE ) and energy performance will be studied (REG) . Prototypes of the valorised products (both organic and plastic ones) will be produced at relevant and industrial relevant environment to demonstrate our solution at TRL6-7, setting the basis for the future industrial implementation. Circular Economy Business Models (CEBMs) and their associated financing mechanisms, for the systemic solutions and technologies demonstrated in the A2C project will be performed (UB, CJM). WP7. A2C systemic solut ion adoption, replication and scalability defines a public engagement specific strategy and implements the required actions to contribute to the acceptance and engagement in the cluster’s transition towards a circular economy, acting as well as a bridge to engage stakeholders at EU level. Community-based innovation schemes will be part of the engagement an d bottom-up approach of A2C bringing . Once the relevant stakeholders are engaged, a specific framework will be established through co-creation to identify, analyse and obtain key information along the different dimensions to merge within the A2C systemic solution. With the data and feedback provided (from research & business models) an A2C systemic solution model will be built, providing the demonstration of the cluster transition as a whol e towards circularity. The model will define key factors of the relevant dimensions for the adoption of A2C systemic solution, with a delivered self-assessment tool (See Figure 7) for defining a roadmap for the replicability and scalability. The replication potential of A2C systemic solution will be demonstrated by studying the replication of the model within specific Italian and Lithuanian regions. P olicy recommendations to improve circular economy related EU and national/local regulation (including standardisation & certification) and recommendations for improving current available financing schemes will be delivered, therefore contributing to the scalability and replicability of the A2C systemic model. Finally, other agrifood clusters and relevant stakeholders already engaged (see Table 44 and support letters) will validate the applicability of A2C systemic solution model and the recommendations derived through cross-fertilization activities. Block 3. Horizontal activities (WP8 & WP9) : WP8. Communication, dissemination, exploitation, training & policy recommendations (Leader:ICONS). Widespreads the project resul ts, engages as many stakeholders as possible, while raising awareness and knowledge transfer about circular economy and innovations produced by A2C. It delivers the A2C D&C strategy and provides the project identity and online channes (ICONS) as well as the content packaging to facilitate the A2C dissemination actions and those contents for addressing the general public outreach (ICONS, all partners). Dedicated actions at local/regional levels will disseminate/communicate and raise awareness while hel p engaging local/regional actors (PRIMA, AGRO,PROEX,CARM,INFO,KVC,ICONS). Intensive networking and clustering action s (A2C partners lead by EURA), at all levels, ensures the widest engagement and visibility of project results while promotes cooperation and engagement with other projetcs/initiatives. Based on WP7 lessons , WP8 delivers a framework for autonomous learning and capacity building in the fields related to A2C at local and EU levels (Leader: UVEG). The long-term sustainability of A2C outcomes will be assured through a comprehensive exploitation strategy and continuous action s on innovation monitoring & market analysis (Leader: ICONS, all partners). Finally, A2C encompasses actions to contribute to the development of new standards. A2C benefits of standardization for increasing the exploitation possibilities (Leader: UNE). WP9. Project management & coordination activities: managed by CETEC, assisting all partners in the ir activites development. Figure 7: A2C model self-assessment tool Back Scope 2 17 Gender dimension: A2C will follow EU gender policy; we regards equal opportunities not just as a legislative requirement but as an ingredient, ensuring there are no inequalities, either mal iciously or unintentionally . A2C strategy lies on three principles: 1) Fostering gender balance in A2C research teams, in order to close the gaps in women participation. 47.9% of personnel involved are women while from all particpants holding a PhD in a science- related discipline, 51.7% are women. 2) Ensuring gender balance in decision-making, in order to reach the target of 40% of the women in panels/groups. The project management board is led by a woman, representing women the 80% of this board main roles. 3) Integrating the gender dimension in research and innovation content: Gender dimension is inherently integrated in our solution and their study and approach will be deeply addressed through the multidimensional contextual analysis from WP7, and project activities/results will be tailored to the detected needs. 1.4. AMBITION 1.4.1. ADVANCE BEYOND THE STATE OF THE ART (SOTA) 1.4.1.1 KEY INNOVATION 1: UPCYCLING OF AGRI-FOOD WASTES 1.4.1.1.1 SOTA: EXTRACTION OF HIGH ADDED VALUE SUBSTANCES FROM AGRIFOOD WASTES Agrifood waste are a valuable source of bioactive compounds 13. Organic s olvent extraction is the conventional method to recover bioactives from vegetal matrix including agrifood wastes, however it has important problems: low selective yield (many other molecules co -extracted with the target ones), environmental unsustainability. To overcome the problems alternative green extraction processes are emerging. Table 6. State of the art of green extraction processes compared to conventional organic solvents extraction Recent research has shown that the combination of properly green extraction methods (hybrid processes) is the best solution to maximize yields while minimizing costs . Promising examples of these hybrid processes are Green Solvents + Ultrasound assisted extraction, Green Solvents + Enzyme assisted extraction and Green Solvents + Microwave assisted extraction and have been tested in a pilot scale by CTNC and SSICA. UAE-EAE14, GS+MAE15 are promising. Limitations: industrial implementation of hybrid green extraction still requires adaptations and fine- tuning operations such as those included in A2C project. ► A2C beyond SOTA. A2C will develop specific green extraction routes searching an optimal use of green solvents, process conditions, energy consumption, process time & enhancement of the extraction yields. These routes will combine the advantages of the hybrid processes:-Route 1. Green solvents + ultrasounds assisted extraction - UAE (target yield 60%) will be applied to obtain dietary fiber, phenolic extracts & carotenoids with a target purity of 70%. -Route 2. Green solvents + enzymatic hydrolisis assisted extraction-EAE+ microwaves assisted extraction- MAE (target yield 70%) will be applied to obtain dietary fiber , and phenolic extracts & carotenoids with a target purity of 80%. The first advance of all our extraction processes will be based on an extraction using non-organic solvents. We will work with aqueous solutions used with the incorporation of enzymes, acidic and basic solutions (such as NaOH/urea aqueous solution), ionic liquids (ILs) composed of organic cations, such as imidazolium, ammonium, and phosphonium and deep eutectic solvents (DEPs) based on quaternary ammonium salts (choline chloride, amines, sugars, alcohols & carboxylic acids) , which have shown good results in the extraction of phenolic compounds, carotenoids and fibres16. The second advance will be the utilisation of hybrid processes for all defined ro utes. This strategy will make it possible to demonstrate the technical and economic industrial viability of the processes by enabling significant energy and timesaving’s and higher extraction yields. The following Table 7 shows the advantages of A2C routes compared to conventional routes. 13 Santana-Méridas O. et al. A2C routes compared to conventional extractions In addition, we will use advanced purification technologies to eliminate any potential impurities in the extracts: membrane filtration for the fibres purification and membranes filtration and adsorption for carotenoids and phenolic. 1.4.1.1.2 SOTA: NEW FOOD, NUTRACEUTICALS & COSMETICS FROM AGRIFOOD WASTES A wide array of studies 17 have demonstrated the potential of different extracts from agri -food wastes for the enrichment of food formulations as natural alternatives to synthetic additives (Table 8). Table 8. Bioactive extracts from agrifood wastes as alternative to conventional additives Furthermore, natural compounds may also be regarded as nutraceutical ingredients18 for products with enhanced nutritional value, health benefits, and longer shelf-life, highlighting phenolics compounds and carotenoids ( high antioxidant potential), and dietary fibers (digestive properties). Moreover, some agrifood wastes extracts have been demonstrated for their cosmetic activity (carotenoids from pineapple) or already employed in cosmetic formulations (such as commercially available lycopene from tomato extraction or essential oils from citrus) 19. But many others could be proposed for further applications, where less effective & more expensive products are currently being used. Limitations: synthetic additives are still usually applied due to their high stability and low cost compared to natural alternative substances. ► A2C beyond SOTA. Some of the higher agrifood wastes generated in Murcia (Spain) (citrus, apples, grapes, artichoke, cauliflower & broccoli) have a great potential to be valorised since they contain a wide variety of bioactive compounds (Table 1) of interest for the food, cosmetic and nutraceutics industries. A2C will devel op innovative food, cosmetic & nutraceutics formulations based on the bioactive compounds extracted from these wastes. Fibres extracted from wastes can find potential applications in food & nutraceutics application as a low -calorie bulking agent useful as a flour or fat replacer or to improve water and oil absorption. Phenolic compounds & carotenoids will be used as powerful antioxidants in food, cosmetic and nutraceutics formulations. A2C will design a specific strategy to achieve a high stability of the phenolic compounds and carotenoids in the new food, nutraceutics & cosmetic formulations since both of them are very sensitive to the de gradation induced by heat, light, oxygen, acid, transition metal, or interactions with radical species. The first step of our strategy will be focused on the identification of the predominant carotenoid/phenolic degradation pathway, and secondly based on it we will design specific delivery systems, taking into account: i ) presence of iron and radicals . We will add a chelator to bind the iron, partitioning it away from the carotenoids/phenolics and making the iron less reactive. Radi cals presence is associated to lipid oxidation, then to limit the radicals presence, our delivery system will include free radical scavengers; ii) contact light prevention to avoid photodegradation; iii) the carotenoid/phenolic ingredient can be easily dis persed into multiple types of food products. iv) the delivery system will not alter the appearance, flavour, or texture of the final product; v) the bioactivity and bioavailability of the carotenoid/phenolic, and any desire to control the release in the final product. -For phenolics & carotenoids to be used as functional food ingredients we will design emulsions specific delivery systems. Based on SSICA experience, A2C will focused on the design of systems with the potential to offer multiple forms of carotenoid/phenolics protection, while still allowing for easy incorporation into foods: oil-in-water emulsions since carotenoids & phenolic substances are lipid -soluble, multilayer emulsions produced by forming layers around an oil droplet, and solid-lipid particles. -For phenolics & carotenoids as functional nutraceutics & cosmetics , SSICA will encapsulate them by ionic gelation, by extruding an aqueous solution of polymer through a syringe needle or a nozzle. 17 Ben-Othman et al. Cosmetics, 2, 82-92, 2015 19 1.4.1.2 KEY INNOVATION 2: UPCYCLING OF MULTILAYER PLASTICS WASTES 1.4.1.2.1 SOTA: SORTING OF MULTILAYER MATERIALS At the recovery facilities, flexible films are separated from rigid heavy items by two well -known technologies (ballistic and air separation). In order to identify the different lightweight materials specific systems could be used afterwards as is shown in Table 9. However, these identification processes and technologies are highly inefficient or prohibitively expensive, especially in case of plastic -metal mixtures. Therefore, many recyclers are still sorting plastics by hand, which is unreliable and expensive. Technology Characteristics Limitations Conventional NIR20 Optical detection (wavelengths reflected by the material after NIR light illumines its surface). Allows sorting of monomaterials (PE, PP, PET) non -black packaging with thickness up to 1mm. Mainly use for separation of rigid plastic packaging and in some cases sorting of flexible PE. Industrially implemented. No efficiency in multilayers. No identification of opaque layers such as aluminium, thin coating layers (e.g. PVdC), thick layers. The equipment cannot distinguish between surface and reverse printing. PELLENC technology21 NIR advance sorting of PE/PA films and PET/PA/PET bottles. Industrially implemented. No identification of opaque layers. No identification of common materials in multilayers (EVOH, Al). REFLEX22 NIR advance sorting of PET/PE and PP/PE films. Demonstrated, at research stage. Low separation efficiencies achieved for flexible packaging. No identification of opaque layers. No identification of common materials in multilayers (EVOH, PA, Al). No identification of complex multilayers (>3 layers). Watermarks23/ Tracers Add digitally marks (recognizable by optical) on the surface of the films during their productions by mechanical actions or printing (HolyGrail 24 has achieve the sorting of monomaterials by this technology)/ Other technology is based on the use of fluorescent tracer materials identifiable by NIR25. Demonstrated, at research stage. No validated in embossed plastics. Most films to sort are, deformed, or fold difficult for the recognition of the watermarks. AI based vision26 Well-developed technology that has been studying in the application of plastics sorting: sorting designs based on AI exhibit promising separation capabilities. Demonstrated for packaging (efficiency 90%). First pilot experiences in USA: spider -like robotic arms guided by cameras and artificial intelligence (AI) implemented to make municipal recycling facilities (MRFs) run more efficiently27. High database needed. No demonstrated for films. HSI Combines the advantages of spectroscopy (rapidity, non -destructive nature), with the possibility of analysing the spatial distribution of quality parameters of multi -materials, by using imaging technology282930. High accuracy demonstrated for films. Not able to detect black and aluminium. Limitations: No technology allows the detection of most used multilayers such as PE/EVOH or complex multilayers containing aluminium. ► A2C beyond SOTA. A2C will go beyond the state of the art by adopting HSI/LIBS in combination with Artificial Intelligent and Machine Learning to address the current detection limitations and improve the sorting efficiency, increasing the feasibility of the identification pr ocess in multi -layer materials inc luding also metallic layers (aluminium). Our partner IRIS has demonstrated the ability to use HSI-NIR for identifying the different layers in laminated plastic films as well as laser induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS31) for identifying black plastics in the H2020 Multicycle project32. While existing point sensor can be applied to large packaging, an imaging technique such as HSI is preferable for multi-material identification of small films or shredded material fed on a belt. Lastly, HSI technology is not able to detect metal (aluminium) layers or black plastics, and will be complemented by the additional spectroscopic technique laser induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS) that will be able to classify dark materials until 99% of accuracy. To reach sufficient accuracy and speed in th e material detection, the spectroscopic measurements will be complemented with an AI / Machine learning statistical evaluation , implemented using a custom -built state -of-the-art platform. These solutions will be validated at a waste sorting facility (GWC), in a belt (yield of at least 25kg/hour) and a sorting efficiency of 80% from the indeed. 1.4.1.2.2 SOTA OF DECONTAMINATION OF PLASTICS WASTES Contamination hinders the reuse of recycled plastics in several applications, such as the food industry . Common decontamination processes applied in the recycling plants consist of a process known as 'triple rinsing and drying' (plastic waste passed through 3 consecutive washes & drying cycles using huge amounts of wastewater and high energy consume). In the case of recycled plastics in contact with food33 the possible migration of chemicals (detergents, solvents or incidental contaminations) is an important issue. Agricultural plastic films contain high contamination by pesticides . Some studies have ide ntified 134 substances including volatile and semi -volatile compounds in recycled LDPE and HDPE 34, being the main groups additives, and NIAs35 (polymer and additives breakdown products & contamination from external sources). Moreover, microplastics , can be unintentionally formed36 (microplastics are not currently eliminated at the recycling plants going across the value chain). A particular problem of plastics waste is the presence of odorous constituents, that are typically not only found on the outer surface, but are also adsorbed deep into the polymer, which make them hard to remove37. In the following table are shown the most advance technologies existing for plastics decontamination. 20 Near Infrared (NIR) sorting is used commonly in the waste industry to recover materials of value from mixed streams. The technology uses differences in the wavelengths of infrared light that is reflected by polymers with different chemical structures. You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe the governance structure, decision-making processes, risk management, and quality assurance.
Write the Management structure and procedures section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

3.2 Management structure and procedures  
The overall aim of our project management is to ensure a successful execution of our project to meet its 
objectives. To achieve this, we, the project manager and our partners, will undertake all the necessary management 
and supporting actions to ensure high quality of our outcomes within the predefined time schedule and budget 
of our project.  
3.2.1 Organisational Structure and Decision making   
Our organisational structure is characterised by the following constitutional elements (fig. 18):   
  We, the Coordinator   
  We, the Work Package and Task Leaders   
  We, the Contractual Partners forming the “Project Consortium and Decision Board”   
  We, the Experts forming the “AI-CUBE Expert Group”   
  We, the Stakeholders’ Community forming the “AI-CUBE European Process Industry Network”   
   
Figure 18. AI-CUBE Structural elements  
Source: Own elaboration  
We, the Coordinator  
We will ensure a smooth and successful implementation of our project. We are responsible for the execution of 
the activities and the overall achievement of our project’s objectives and thus for all contractual obligations 
towards the Commission. We may delegate specific subjects to our partners without however sharing our partners’ 
responsibility. Our coordinating organisation provides the secretarial and organisational service for our project, 
including contractual and financial issues, reporting, the coordination of input to documents and their finalising, 
dissemination, or delivery, preparation of project meetings, the setting up and running of an infrastructure for 
internal and external communication, and running the everyday business of our project. We will report on a 
regular basis to the EC (Project Officer, PO).  
Project management and coordination will be carried out according to the highest quality, transparency and 
efficiency standards, and will be under our responsibility at PNO, which will make use of its experience and 
expertise from the successful management of and participation in multiple EU funded projects, such as the 
CSAs INSPIRE, NEXT-NET, New_Innonet and BioLinX, the RTD ROBOX, the RIA MEMERE, Indus3ES, 
in H2020, CSAs BIOTIC, R4R, CRM_INNONET, the IAs DESTINY and REE4EU and many others in 7FP. 
We will be led by Ronald Weerdmeester, European Innovation Manager, with more than 20 years’ 
experience in managing EU funded projects and advisor to both CEFIC, Suschem and SPIRE. He will work in 
tandem with Chiara Eleonora De Marco, Innovation Consultant, and other sectorial experts within PNO.  
The Work Package and Task Leaders   
We, the work package and task leaders, are contractual partners responsible for a work package or a task 
respectively. We have responsibility for the preparation and execution of the activities in our work packages or 
tasks according to our work plan. We may delegate specific sub-tasks to our partners without however giving 
up the responsibility for the overall work package or task. We are also responsible for providing general 
guidelines and interfacing with and for coordinating work with other work packages or tasks, in particular in 
meeting the deadlines for results delivery as these deliverables are needed as input for tasks to be timely 
performed in other work packages. We are also responsible for regular reporting on the work progress to the 
rest of our Project Consortium and Coordinator. In case of major developments that may impact the overall 
work plan, or problems in carrying out the tasks, we need to inform the Coordinator as soon as possible and 
discuss with him procedural and strategic issues in order to take common decisions to reach our project’s 
objectives.   
The Contractual Partners forming the “Project Consortium and Decision Board”   
We, the contractual partners forming our project consortium, encompass all staff from our partner organisations 
involved in our project. We contribute to the achievement of the AI-CUBE CSA and accept that we are 
responsible for the tasks assigned to us according to the contract between our consortium and the European 
Commission. More specifically, we:   
• participate in regular meetings,   
• contribute to reports/deliverables,   
• cooperate in elaborating procedures and the work to be carried out in our project,   
• implement and carry out the activities as outlined in our work programme, mobilising the necessary 
resources and building up contacts with third parties as far as necessary,   
• submit and review milestones and deliverables according to the responsibilities defined and agreed 
within our consortium and according to the quality assurance procedure,   
• inform each other - and the coordinator, in particular - on all relevant events and activities related to 
the INSPIRE project,   
• deliver cost statements and input to project activity and financial reports and provide information and 
data to the coordinator in order to ensure timely elaboration and submission of documents,   
• collaborate and contribute to the elaboration of documents, tools and material,  
• carry out dissemination and promotion activities and represent our project externally.   
Our consortium of AI-CUBE is small and involves only 5 partners. Together we will form the Project 
Decision Board, which is the actual decision-making entity of the CSA; i.e. the place where procedural and 
strategic issues are discussed and decided in order to reach our project’s objectives. Decisions may regard 
strategic, general and operational issues, e.g. the implementation of procedures and structures; the definition 
of activities to be carried out, the allocation and re-allocation of budget (in accordance with the EU contract); 
the management of conflicts and the monitoring of procedures and activities, and the inclusion of additional 
Associated Partners. One representative duly nominated by each partner organisation participates in the Project 
Decision Board. Should the nominated representative not be able to participate, he/she will nominate a deputy. 
Regular Project Decision Board meetings will be held quarterly, either physically or remotely - and according to 
necessity. Detailed decision-making processes within our consortium will be defined in the Consortium 
Agreement. Decisions will be taken by a simple majority vote. The voting will be open (non-secret), with one 
vote per partner.  
The Experts forming the “AI-CUBE Expert Group”  
This Expert Group will gather recognised experts in the various domains that are key to Europe’s process 
industry and digital technology sector. They will participate in the AI-CUBE processes through consultation 
and validation processes and will be asked to review the related results and deliverables. They will also be 
given the opportunity to contribute to the results by delivering input to AI-CUBE activities of assessment and 
investigation of AI&BD technology deployment in the European process industry. The experts will represent a 
broader community of specialists in Europe and beyond. International experts to be invited to our Expert Group 
and already identified within the individual networks of AI-CUBE partners are Mr. Jacques Komornicki, 
representing the European Technology Platform SusChem; and Prof. Da. Ir. Greet Van Eetvelde at the Ghent 
University, representing the SPIRE EPOS project (Enhanced energy and resource Efficiency and Performance

1 Excellence  
1.1 Objectives and ambition  
1.1.1 Context and strategic objectives  
We are aware that the building sector is responsible for 40% of energy consumption and 36% of CO2 emissions in the EU. The 2018 Energy Performance of Buildings Directive (EPBD) and the 2012 Energy Efficiency Directive (EED), the main EU’s legislation regarding reduction of energy consumption of buildings, have set the target that all new buildings must be nearly Zero Energy Buildings (nZEB) by 31st December 2020 [1]. Moreover, Art. 5 in the EED has set a 3% annual renovation target for public buildings owned and occupied by the central government from the beginning of 2014 onwards. Central government buildings must be renovated to meet at least the national minimum energy performance requirements set in application of Art. 4 of the EPBD. With the ambition to become the world’s first climate-neutral continent by 2050, the European Commission presented on 11 December 2019 the European Green Deal. Among the proposed measures, the European Green Deal includes the ambition “engaging in a ‘renovation wave’ of private and public buildings”. Thus, as nZEB becomes the new standard, the role of building materials and embodied energy or related CO2-emission is becoming more and more important. We recognize that a focus on energy efficiency only entails a clear risk of having buildings not necessarily performing very well with respect to other environmental criteria [2,3]. Also, the EC has identified the importance of building materials when it states that: “The construction and use of buildings in the EU account for about half of all our extracted materials and energy consumption and about a third of our water consumption. The sector also generates about one third of all waste and is associated with environmental pressures that arise at different stages of a building's life-cycle including the manufacturing of construction products, building construction, use, renovation and the management of building waste” [4].  

Our Exploit4InnoMat OITB (or Ecosystem) aspires to provide a sustainable solution across Europe for the design, upscaling and validation of new material concepts for the building envelope that will facilitate in the achievement of the EC target for nZEB. We will provide services across the supply chain of materials, starting from the design and upscaling of them, and going to installation, validation and assessment of scenarios for the end of life. The core of our Exploit4InnoMat is the 9 Pilot Lines, situated in 7 EU countries, able to process different types of advanced and/or nano-enabled multifunctional materials, including sorting and processing of construction and demolition wastes (CDW) to be used as secondary raw materials. Our target is to develop sustainable materials and products that not only will reduce the operational energy demand (e.g. by providing better insulation properties, reducing thermal bridges, and harvesting or storage of energy) but will also have lower embodied energy (e.g. due to the use of CDW or other secondary raw materials and optimizing their production process), providing a healthier and more comfortable indoor environment (thermal comfort, cleaner air). In order to achieve this target, we have brought a wide range of expertise to the OITB covering fields such as materials characterization and modelling, monitoring and process control, environmental assessment, regulatory and standardization, social acceptance and innovation management.  

Our Exploit4InnoMat project focuses on the development of a sustainable open innovation test bed (OITB) consisting of 6 Groups of services offered to SMEs or Industry. The first Group contains the 9 pilot plants distributed across Europe (Norway, Sweden, Germany, Spain, Greece, Italy and Belgium) dedicated to the development of materials and components for building’s envelope. The other five groups will offer technical and non-technical services (e.g. materials characterization and modelling, validation of products in living hubs, regulatory and standardization consultancy and innovation management) supporting the Pilot Lines. Figure 1.1 provides a schematic of our Exploit4InnoMat OITB.  

The Exploit4InnoMat OITB will provide our services through a Single Entry Point (SEP), a new legal entity (joint venture) that we will found at month 12 of our project. The SEP will undertake the whole administrative effort for signing contracts on behalf of our OITB members with our clients. In this way, a one-stop shop will be formed, facilitating our clients' access to the Pilot Lines and the other services of the OITB. We target strengthening the cooperation between various stakeholders (e.g. technology and service providers, SMEs, large enterprises) and developing new business models for the construction sector, based on Open Innovation Philosophy.  

1.1.2 Implementations Objectives, Expected Results and Measurable Outputs  
The objectives of our Exploit4InnoMat are in accordance with the challenges raised by the topic NMBP-05-2020 and will be implemented through building an OITB comprising a group of Pilot lines and fast prototyping units, together with 5 groups of services (Characterization & Environmental Assessment, Modelling, Validation Sites, Regulatory & Standardization, Innovation management). Our target for the OITB is to assist innovative concepts to reach the market and thus, contribute to the targets set by the EC for achieving significant reduction of energy consumption in buildings (nZEB). Increased profitability will result from our collaboration between experts in different technology and services fields, while shortened time to market, especially for SMEs that have a lack of resources for R&D activities, will allow us to reduce our financial asset commitment and therefore enhance our flexibility. 3.2.1 Consortium as a whole  
We present a highly interconnected network of partners that will act towards the sustainable development of three different end user applications, which have been selected due to the fact that they represent a wide spectrum of sectors interested in our project results. We are distributed across 5 Countries (Italy, Spain, Greece, Israel, and Turkey). We will have a unique role in the consortium and synergies will be created between us across the project implementation. Our consortium is characterized as highly industrial driven, having in our partnership 6 SMEs including the coordinator, 3 Large Enterprises, and 3 Universities. More specifically:  
Gaser: An SME in the north of Italy, a member of a group of companies acting in the field of surface finishing. Gaser has a long tradition in the development of proprietary technologies via in-house research activities. It has a long-standing collaboration with PoliMi and has collaborated closely with Cnano. Its role will be vertical spanning from the synthesis of chemical baths up to the application of the FreeMe technologies and validation of coatings.  
Cnano is a Greek SME that is well known for the optimization of industrial surface treatment processes, offering multiple solutions for everyday troubleshoots of plating facilities and developing customized plating methods. The company has been working for the last 5 years on 3D Printing, developing PoP protocols and plating baths formulations. Cnano is also active in the art & design sector, collaborating with famous artists (i.e. Costas Gaitis) for applying coatings in artifacts. Finally, Cnano has research agreements with Gaser and PoliMi.

3.2 Capacity of participants and consortium as a whole Our consortium reflects a balanced choice of relevant stakeholders in the EU water scene, facilitating the multiactor engagement process. We, the H2OforAll consortium, involve 18 participants from 10 different countries (Portugal, Spain, Poland, Israel, United Kingdom, Germany, Sweden, Cyprus, Netherlands, and Belgium), including 11 Universities/Research Institutes (UC, UULM, ULeicester, CYENS, UHaifa, Ugdansk, KTH, UNIVLEEDS, Utwente, IVL, WETSUS) and 7 Companies from 4 European countries (DHVMED, IRIS Technology Solutions, SPI, Adventech, Water Europe, AtlantiumTLTD, AC). Our research team is composed of 43% female and 57% male investigators. Several synergies are expected to result from different interactions that we will establish between the involved beneficiaries and associated partners due to their different and complementary backgrounds, as shown in Figure 3.3. UC is the main proponent of our project, and we will be responsible for its coordination and management, in close collaboration with all the participants (WP1). Also, UC has a strong background in water treatment technologies development as well as water characterization. We will work in close collaboration with researchers from WE and UNIVLEEDS on the dissemination and communication of the main achievements that will result from each WP and getting people (from academia, industry, and the general public) involved in drinking water management issues and pollutants mitigation solutions. We have previously demonstrated experience in designing and developing materials appropriate and effective for communicating results and outcomes of EC projects to the widest range of relevant stakeholders (participated as a coordinator or partner in over 25 FP7/CIP and 45 H2020 projects). UNIVLEEDS, involving social sciences scientists, has large experience in participatory crowd-sensing approaches for increasing citizens’ awareness and engaging with policy-making stakeholders (e.g., development of collective decision-making platforms such as Smart-Agora, EPOS, etc.). UULM will bring knowledge in technologies for sensing persistent molecular pollutants in aquatic systems. IRIS is an advanced engineering company, certified under ISO 9001:2008 and UNE-EN-ISO 166002, specialized in process monitoring, control, and optimization. UT has a strong expertise in the development of sensing infrastructures for the monitoring of water quality. Wetsus will bring the know-how on the cost-effective and high-resolution methods for detecting the selected molecules and the materials they interact with. CYENS develops algorithms and methods to model water distribution networks to detect vulnerabilities to water contamination threats for selecting the most relevant monitoring. Besides this, we will allow to shed light regarding DBPs formation pathways, fate along the distribution network as well as their interaction with pipes and naturally occurring materials. DHV brings the know-how regarding drinking water prevention strategies and regulation. ULeic has a strong expertise in toxicity assessment through MCDM and will help prioritize the most dangerous DBPs as well as evaluate the novel water treatment/disinfection technologies proposed. The company Atlantium has strong expertise in UV disinfection technologies that will complement/substitute the current approaches. UHaifa will explore the simultaneous adsorption and oxidation process for pollutants abatement from source water and DBPs removal from drinking water. KTH will apply their know-how in magnetic bio-activated carbons from biomass waste to remove contaminants from water while easily recovering the adsorbent. UGdansk has large experience in photocatalytic water treatments and photo-materials preparation that will be used to degrade contaminants from water and drinking water. Since our project aims at the employment of green and sustainable technologies/solutions/materials within a circular economy concept, specialists from Adventech will help to identify synergies between all the materials/processes developed and optimized and select the most suitable ones for scale-up and to build a fully automated transportable lab-pilot scale unit that will be tested under semi-industrial conditions. IVL will assess and compare the LCA and LCC of the novel integrated water treatment and disinfection technologies compared with the conventional approaches to reach the most cost-effective and sustainable methodology. Water Europe is the recognized voice and promoter of water-related RTD and innovation in Europe. With over 250 members across Europe on board, we are recognized by the European Commission as the industry-led European Technology Platform (ETP) for Water. Leveraging from our extensive network and experience in projects, we will play an important role in the dissemination and policy work packages aiming to maximize the impact of our project activities and results. The utility municipal company Águas de Coimbra (AC) manages the water distribution network of Coimbra city and will provide water samples, process water quality data, perform hydraulic and quality modeling through the network, support the field demonstration of the developed sensing infrastructure, and promote citizens' awareness and engagement on water quality. Our consortium has a high international background (involving countries all around Europe and researchers with a high degree of internationalization) with a multidisciplinary (Chemical, Electro, Civil and Environmental Engineers, Chemistry, Biology, Biochemistry, Sociologists) team with the required skills and infrastructures (as described in Part A of the proposal) to fully develop our work plan. It is worthy to notice that some of our participants have already been involved in joint projects creating bonds of trust and the willingness to work together again and establish new opportunities to advance in science and contribute to an effective transition. An effective share of the critical infrastructures and samples will occur by researchers’ interchange among us in a cross-fertilizing environment. We cover the value chain from the product/technology development, through the relation with citizens, stakeholders, and policy-makers all the way to the potential commercialization (bearing in mind the end-users and companies involved in our consortium). All the members of our consortium were already involved in international and national/regional projects (see selected projects at Part A) involving open science dissemination and have demonstrated expertise and competence in those practices. An Advisory Board (AB) is constituted for our project; we have already the support of Águas do Centro Litoral that manages the municipal wastewater treatment plants and drinking water production in the Centre Region of Portugal. This company will be part of the AB and will provide water samples for testing the water treatment and disinfection processes that we will develop in H2OforAll. The institute Waterschap Vechtstromen and the Netherlands Institute of Ecology also agreed to be part of our advisory board. Water Europe members will be contacted also to be part of the AB. This board will join us in three of the meetings – M6, M18, and M30, in order to provide feedback about our project achievements and suggest improvements that can increase the impact of our project results. Milestone 1.1 defines month 3 to have the AB constituted with at least 5 institutions. However, we are committed to enlarging the AB with organizations that may bring added value to the meeting discussions. In these meetings, representatives of other projects in correlated areas can also be invited. H2OforAll has all the required critical mass, infrastructures, and know-how to fully develop our work plan, giving a strong contribution to enhance the competitiveness of the EU water sector and fostering the EU’s position and role in the global water scene while promoting water protection.

Project: 101138473— IS2H4C – Part B – 5  

1.  EXCELLENCE    Associated with document Ref. Ares(2023)8308660 - 05/12/2023  

Heavily industrialized clusters are important sources of jobs and wealth in the EU. However, despite our ongoing efforts of EU policies, we are lagging in the implementation of Industrial Symbiosis (IS) to transit to more resource- and energy-efficient setups. Furthermore, citizens living in the clusters' proximity suffer from a polluted environment. Despite the EU's strategic priorities in sustainable development, human-centred IS awaits to be designed, implemented, and expanded. Currently, the state-of-the-art of IS falls short of such a demand. Initiatives like SPIRE (now Processes for Planet: P4Planet) have researched and demonstrated pilot concepts of IS and even Industrial-Urban-Rural Symbiosis (IURS), achievable via closing energy, resource, and data loops through infrastructure sharing. In this regard, P4Planet has designed the concept Hubs for Circularity (H4C)1. H4C provide spaces where diverse actors, such as businesses, governments, researchers, and civil society, collaborate to accelerate the Circular Economy (CE) transition. They are nexuses for knowledge and resource sharing, cross-industrial collaboration, and sustainable technology innovation. H4C can take various forms, from physical spaces, such as IS Networks (ISNs) and co-working clusters, to virtual platforms such as online communities and digital marketplaces. They can also serve as a resource for governmental bodies, providing evidence-based research and recommendations for CE policymaking. Overall, H4C pave the way for IS leading to a net-zero CE. In light of advanced IS implementation, our "From Industrial Symbiosis to Hubs for Circularity: IS2H4C” project will deliver a systemic approach of IS to Hubs for Circularity, helping P4Planet materialise its goals.  
Next to conventional IS achieved through the exchange of materials, water, and energy, we promote infrastructure-, service, and carbon-based IS to transform existing industrial areas into ISNs. These ISNs are integrated with surrounding rural and/or urban ecosystems to form physical H4C. The development of physical H4C is facilitated by a digital collaboration platform (Digital H4C). Digital H4C fosters the bottom-up emergence of ISNs by real-time resource monitoring and tracking, dynamic supply-demand matchmaking, efficient and secure information-sharing, and transparent marketplace trading. It also performs effective evaluation of top-down coordination strategies based on a multi-dimensional approach combining attributional and consequential Life Cycle Assessment (LCA). We demonstrate four first-of-a-kind hubs with business cases and exploitation strategies in the Netherlands, Germany, Spain, and Turkey, populated by various industrial sectors (Fig. 1). Cutting-edge green technologies are deployed and demonstrated to realise IS in demo hubs, including wind and solar power generation, electrolysis for hydrogen, carbon capture and utilisation, waste heat capture and distribution, heat pumps, hydrogen use in built environment, and oxycombustion. The technology innovation and integration elevate the sustainable industrial performance and boost the socioeconomic wellbeing in the surrounding ecosystems. In a nutshell, IS2H4C is a real-life demonstrator of H4C in scale. It establishes best IS practices towards a climate neutral, circular, and digitised future.

1.1. OBJECTIVES AND AMBITION    Associated with document Ref. Ares(2023)8308660 - 05/12/2023  
Our mission is to pave the way towards the development of H4C based on the circularity and resilience requirements of existing and future industrial zones and their surrounding ecosystems. To achieve this mission, we prioritize resource efficiency, use of renewable energy, and waste prevention, and promote IURS via reuse and recycling of unavoidable solid, liquid, and gas waste streams via deployment of innovative sustainable technologies and via systemic integration of existing and new infrastructure. Importantly, we ensure societal and governmental engagement to H4C via securing acceptance and trust among citizens based on clear communication, transparency, and inclusion, by proposing sustainable business and financial models, as well as respecting the planetary health boundaries. Our biggest ambition is to become a reference for promoting H4C as Europe’s future sustainable regional development model.  
We formulated six operational objectives with specific Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) to precisely monitor the technological readiness evolution, our project’s advancement beyond the State of the Art (SoA), and to operationally implement IS2H4C's mission.  

Objective 1. Deploy and demonstrate near commercial scale industrial symbiosis through innovative technologies  
Each hub has strong individualized and already-implemented technologies that aim to comply with the EU Green Deal guidelines2. We scale up and integrate recent sustainable technologies (at different TRLs) to the involved industries and infrastructure to exploit the hubs' vast room for resource and energy-use optimisation and IURS implementation. We demonstrate how H4C emerge from bottom-up3, operate, and evolve in four locations (populated with different process industries and rural/urban combinations) and bring these H4C to a systemic TRL7/TRL8. We propose two types of synergies: (1) 17 synergies technologically demonstrated in the project and (2) 24 synergies to be studied for hub expansion in the project. Furthermore, scale-up and further expansion will be supported by on-site matchmaking workshops and IS recommenders embedded in the digital H4C via deploying Information Technologies. For the first category, we propose the following technology and infrastructure deployment and implementations in the involved H4C:  
1. Basque Hub: contains oil refining, steel, pulp and paper and lime industries, and the public wastewater treatment plant located in the highly industrialized area of the Basque Country. Planned synergies: (i) oxycombustion with O2 and H2 in the steel industry (potentially from the forthcoming electrolysis of the oil refining sector). (ii) CO2 via carbon capture (CC) by the lime industry and used (CCU) to produce CH4 (and potentially for the forthcoming production of e-fuels in the oil sector). (iii) Carbonation of steel slags with the CO2 obtained from the lime industry (CCU) to produce construction products for the cement industry.  
2. Dutch Hub: Twente area contains a small-scale hydrogen hub formed by approximately 20 process industry companies connected to a nearby village, Aadorp, which is planned to evolve into a positive energy district via becoming independent from natural gas and fossil-based electricity. Planned synergies: (i -ii-iii) Green H2 obtained from electrolysis based on solar energy and (to-be-implemented) wind energy is used in five houses in built environment in Aadorp via (to-be-implemented) H2 pipelines. (iv) Energy storage is implemented to stabilize the feed of the green energy generation mentioned in synergies i-ii to tackle supply uncertainty. (v) H2 is transported to three crematoria in Twente by H2 tube trailers & substitutes natural gas. (vi-vii) O2 from electrolysis will be sent to the wastewater treatment unit located 100 meters away from the electrolyser for water purification & treated water will be sent to electrolyser for H2 production via (to-be-implemented) two pipelines.  
3. Turkish Hub: Izmir-Manisa area contains petrochemical and household appliances companies, located nearby an industrialized port area on the Aegean coast. Planned synergies: (i -ii) Green H2 obtained from electrolysis based on the use of renewable energy and captured CO2 from oil refining activities via CC adsorption technology are used for e-methanol production. (iii-iv) Captured CO2 is also used in the production of non-isocyanate polyurethane (NIPU) which substitutes traditional polyurethane in the production of refrigerators.

Two project management bodies are envisaged (see Fig. 17): The Executive Board (EB), which is an executive management body of our project, and the General Assembly (GA), which is the strategic steering and decision-making body. Day-to-day decision making will be the responsibility of the Executive board, chaired by us, the Coordinator, whose membership will comprise the Coordinator (chair), Scientific Manager (Deputy Coordinator), Project manager and WP leaders. The Executive board will meet as frequently as required with a minimum of every 6 months. In general, occasions will be sought to combine activities to minimise travel costs. The decision-making body on major strategic decisions will be the General Assembly, chaired by us, the Coordinator, whereby each partner organisation will appoint one representative. The GA will in addition include us, the Coordinator (chair), the Scientific Manager, the Project Manager, the Exploitation Manager, the Innovation Manager, and the Dissemination Manager. Each partner is assigned one vote to ensure a democratically run project (2/3 majority voting). Major strategic decisions will be made by the General Assembly, such as change of scope, IPR related decisions, conflicts, budget related decisions, etc. All matters of a strategic nature will be floated to the General Assembly and will aim to be accompanied by a recommendation from the Executive board. The GA suggests refocusing of research directions, proposes the publication plan and decides on conflicting issues such as prioritizing samples in case of a temporary breakdown or other contingency situation and subsequent mitigating actions (see table 3.2b). The General Assembly will meet once per year.  
In addition, a voluntary Advisory Board has been established that will provide guidance and advice to our consortium through its international experts in themes of high relevance for our project. To this end, the Advisory Board (AB) of PHOTONFOOD involves two experts from the field of photonics (Prof. Gottfried Strasser from TU Vienna, Austria, expert in light sources and Prof. Goran Mashanovich from University of Southampton, an expert in waveguides). IBM as a global cloud platform and cognitive solutions company represented by Loek Vredenberg will be part of our Advisory Board to engage with their blockchain based Food Trust platform and supporting skills. IBM will contribute to developing an interface between our PHOTONFOOD software and the IBM Food Trust platform for integrating data and utilizing data for improving food safety and reducing waste along the food chain. The Directorate General for Health and Food Safety/European commission in Brussels represented by Frans Verstraete will participate in our advisory board as a policy maker organization. The Advisory Board will provide an important mechanism for providing guidance on the General Assembly. It is noteworthy that the Advisory Board is a voluntary body. The Advisory Board will sign a confidentiality agreement with us, the Coordinator, in order to safeguard the confidential aspects of our project that they might be exposed to. The members of the Advisory Board will have no decision-making capacity in our project. The AB will meet three times with the GA. Two times by video meetings and two times in person. A budget for this is foreseen in the NMBU budget. All advisory board members have expressed their willingness to join the Advisory Board (LOIs available, see Annex) should our PHOTONFOOD project be selected for funding. Since our consortium of PHOTONFOOD has a broad coverage of stakeholders that are strongly involved in the user acceptance and validation and demonstration activities, the advisory board was chosen to cover policy maker members and scientific members to have further reality checks for the technological developments in our project.

1  Excellence   
  
Introduction  
Mineral resources are an important material basis for our entire economy. The International Mineralogical Association’s (IMA) members annually produce 180 million tonnes of industrial minerals for the EU-28, Turkey, and Norway. These include calcium carbonate (precipitated and ground), dolomite, lime, bentonite, magnesite, and other valuable minerals. Industrial minerals, either in a processed or natural state, are used to make building materials, ceramics, electronics, glass, medications and medical devices, paints, paper and plastics, and a myriad of other industrial and domestic products, contributing €14 billion to the EU economy. The non-ferrous metals sector provides 500,000 direct jobs and 3 million indirect jobs in the EU, thousands more across wider Europe. These jobs are often high-quality well-paid jobs, and trade unions have come together to fight to keep these jobs in Europe. European trade unions call for urgent action to level the global playing field to ensure our competitiveness in producing high-quality green products, while preserving and creating jobs. Amongst the many processes employed in mineral processing, calcination, a crucial process in the minerals processing and cement industries, involves heating materials to high temperatures. This process is integral to transforming raw materials into useful products and plays a vital role in the efficiency and sustainability of these industries. Roasting is a similar process for heating sulfide ore to a high temperature in the presence of air. It is a step in the processing of certain ores. More specifically, roasting is often a metallurgical process involving gas-solid reactions at elevated temperatures with the goal of purifying the metal component(s). The gaseous product of sulfide roasting, sulfur dioxide (SO2) is often used to produce sulfuric acid. Many sulfide minerals contain other components such as arsenic that are released into the environment. Rotary kilns have become essential in these processes, due to their diverse capabilities and high degree of control over process parameters. While rotary kilns have become the backbone of many new industrial processes that make the world a more efficient and sustainable place, the technology cannot (or often should not) process material in just any shape or form. In addition to being inefficient, feeding unprepared material to a kiln can have several unintended consequences, ranging from an incomplete reaction or a failure to achieve the intended reaction, to unnecessarily long retention times and excessive energy costs. Direct-fired rotary kilns (DFRK) rely on direct contact between the process gas and the material in order to heat the material to the specified temperature, leading to fine and ultrafine particles becoming entrained in the process gas flow and carried out through the exhaust gas system. This results in a high rate of reprocessing and can significantly reduce the overall process efficiency, in addition to other issues. In the cement industry, the amount of cement kiln dust (CKD) generated typically ranges 15–20% of the produced cement, leading to an annual CKD production of approximately 12.9 metric tons. Similarly, the lime industry generates Lime Kiln Dust (LKD), with an annual production of approximately 1.8 to 3.6 million metric tons in the United States only. CKD and LKD are usually stockpiled, and it is estimated that the total amount of kiln dust currently stockpiled throughout the country exceeds close to 90 million metric tons (100 million tons), creating substantial issues in their management. In the critical metal industries, the wide use of carbon as a cheap reduction material as well as the lack of the relevant fundamental knowledge and expertise has limited the hydrogen use to a few cases of refractory metals. However, hydrogen could be used as an alternative to carbon reductant, provided that there is enough fundamental knowledge concerning the thermochemical feasibility and equilibrium, the kinetics, and the factors, i.e. metallic mineral mineralogical phase, affecting the hydrogen/metal oxide/sulfide reaction system. Moreover, it is necessary to deeply understand the technical problems arising in the case of treating complex metal ores and concentrates due to the various compounds and mineralogical phases present in the reaction system. In this context, our PRIM-ROCK project represents a new frontier in the metallurgical industry in view of developing and demonstrating the necessary fundamental knowledge that will pave the way to sustainable, more energy-efficient mineral processes with low carbon footprint, in a significant number of critical metals. Moreover, we will develop entirely new, breakthrough technologies enabling the direct extraction of metals from the EU metal sulfide resources. Our project will also demonstrate an integrated approach to treat critical metal minerals coupled with onsite renewable hydrogen production, powered by artificial intelligence. The knowledge developed in our PRIM-ROCK innovative processes directly targets the real scale application in the relevant industrial areas and will also provide the foundations for going beyond the specific cases studied in our project towards the development of a climate neutral critical metal hydrogen metallurgy on the long term. Both calcination and roasting processes present important optimisation potential dependent on the effective pre-treatment of raw material ores. In the ore sorting process, accurate, machine-assisted classification and sorting, as well as determining the optimal crushing size are key steps, greatly influencing the efficiency and productivity of the production process, while also enabling the utilisation of lower grade ores. For both sectors, process innovations are needed to improve resource efficiency, reduce waste and low-value by-product generation, and mitigate emissions and risk to worker health and safety.
1.1  Objectives and ambition   
  
PRIM-ROCK aims to bring a step-change in the calcination and roasting processes, thanks to a holistic approach centred around ground-breaking process intensification (PI) and digital technologies, considering the following:  
  
Figure 2. Overview of the process line addressing multiple types of raw materials.  
(1) pre-processing stage, referred to in our project as the Raw Material Intelligence (RMI) system (KER1), comprised of a process line featuring advanced sensorisation, AI-driven sorting, and decision-support, complete with real-time analysis capabilities and optimized crushing size determination for the raw materials. KER1 will contribute to process efficiency in a versatile manner, establishing an interface between pre-processing of raw materials and their thermal treatment and (a) optimising pre-processing in accordance to process parameters, (b) enabling the adaptation of process parameters depending on raw material changes, even in real-time. Pre-processing will thus enable the resource efficiency of the corresponding processes to be increased by over 10%.  
(2) replacement of Direct-fired rotary kilns (DFRKs) with Indirect-fired rotary kilns (IFRKs) and microwave rotary kilns (MRKs) as the process components (KER2 – Intensification technologies for thermal processing). Replacing DFRKs with the electrified IFRKs and MRKs removes the need for process gases, solving the issue of fine and ultrafine material loss through drag, reducing emissions and improving the controllability and flexibility of the calcination and roasting processes. The capacity to process without losses of fine and ultrafine material is the major contributor to process efficiency, as losses can exceed 25% in many industries as previously described. Moreover, adoption of the technology removes the need for pelletisation and filters. Moreover, as slag or dam ring formation in the rotary kiln can be avoided, resource efficiency will increase by 6-12%. This refers to a build-up around the circumference of the kiln’s interior in such a way that material is either prevented from passing through or is significantly inhibited. Ring formation is extremely common, occurring in roughly 85% of commercial rotary kilns. It is typically the result of a change in slagging temperature. Thus, depending on the process, KER2 will contribute to resource efficiency in the 20-32% range.  
(3) improvements on post-process component level, enabling us to use the kiln for direct reduction through H2 as a reducing agent, which will be subsequently recovered by decomposition through Microwave-Assisted Plasma (MIP) reactor (KER3 – Post-processing solutions), which requires a sustainable and low-cost H2 supply that is the goal of this activity. Replacement of C by H2 and high levels of recovery will improve the resource efficiency of reduction processes of the selected lines by over 60% (FeNi case, from 45% to 90% to pre-reduction stage). Additionally, an innovative heat exchanger prototype will be developed at the post-process level to manage the thermal quenching effect of the plasma and cool the process gases in a controlled way, maximising recovery. Finally, due to the substantial enriched CO2 produced by calcination, a virtual toolkit for decision support in capture and utilisation in e-fuels will be developed, enabling credible feasibility assessments.  
(4) Enabling the integration of KER1-3 and maximizing the improvements in resource efficiency will be achieved through the digital infrastructure component (KER4 - Digital process-line optimisation). KER4 will deploy tailored, and exhaustive simulations of the pre-process, electrified process and post-process stages to obtain the optimal design of the prototypes, while process digital twins will be developed with data-driven functions for efficient monitoring (with 3D visualization capabilities) and further optimization. An innovative data platform will facilitate data fusion, bridging the models, the Process Control System (PCS) and the advanced sensor network of all stages. 3.2 Capacity of participants and consortium as a whole  
3.2.1. Description of our consortium  
We are a strongly industry-driven Innovation Action, with our consortium composition focused on developing and industrial deployment of circular tarpaulins. Our consortium consists of 12 companies (9 large enterprises (LE) and 3 SMEs), 2 industry associations, and 2 RTD partners. All of us are considered renowned organizations within our respective sectors. Together, these 16 complementary partners form a multidisciplinary team that covers all parts needed for circularity (i.e. from tarp producers over re-use to applicators of recycled and biobased materials). This well-balanced set of competences and organizations is brought together from 8 EU countries (Belgium, Netherlands, Germany, Austria, France, Spain, Italy, and Czech Republic).  
The idea for our project was initiated in 2022 by the 4 largest European tarp producers, SIOEN (BE), SF (FR), MEHLER (DE), and SATTLER (AU), representing over 90% of the EU tarp production. At that time, we defined the need for a European industrial value chain that enhances the circularity of the 200,000 tons per year of post-production and end-of-life tarps. Together we decided to join forces to make what is by now called the RETAIN project a success. We are the core drivers of Figure 8: contribution of participants in the RETAIN project, and our roles are quite diverse: development of a digital product passport for tarps, application of recyclates into novel tarps, re-use and reconfectioning of post-production and end-of-life tarps to enable a valuable, second life for these materials, ecodesign for uptake of recycled and biobased materials, market acceptance, uptake and deployment, and demonstration of processes.  
These 4 tarp producers carefully selected further companies for completion of the industrial value chain needed for circularization, covering the aspects of waste collection, recycling, and plastic converters for the uptake of recycled streams as well as for developing the digital logistics platform.  
For waste collection, we included VEOLIA (FR), one of Europe’s largest waste management companies. With subsidiaries all across the EU, VEOLIA is the ideal partner for us to tackle all logistical challenges that come with the collection and sorting of post-production and end-of-life tarps. We will work in close collaboration with most other partners as they are also coordinating the demonstration work package WP5.  
The recycling industry is strongly involved in our project via 3 complementary recycling partners: VWERVEN (NL), the largest recycler of hard plastics in Europe, including rigid PVC; EKOTREND (CZ), the largest EU recycler of soft PVC plastics; and KKF (DE), a key recycler of rigid and soft PVC. Their complementarity is demonstrated by their respective RETAIN tasks: VWERVEN will focus on the gate control process, more specifically on (semi)automized deconfectioning and washing of tarps, EKOTREND on grinding/shredding, slicing/shaving, granulation, and analytical techniques, and KKF on mechanical separation techniques. Together we will also execute tasks related to the fast characterization of legacy additives.  
From the converters side, we have two strong large industry partners: BIG (BE), world leader in the use of plasticized PVC for flooring applications, and RENO (DE), world leader for roofing and pool applications. We will test and demonstrate the use of recycled and biobased products in these applications. This work will be executed in WP4 (Ecodesign using recycled and biobased materials to produce high-value products, and related standardization aspects).

ARCESSO contributes expertise and equipment in PU processing.

3.2 Capacity of participants and consortium as a whole We, the CO2HESION consortium, consist of a complementary team of research, technology, industrial, and innovation ecosystem partners covering the full value chain required to develop and validate an integrated CO2 capture, purification, and utilisation (CCU) system for the production of competitive chemical products. We combine expertise in carbon capture, catalytic conversion, reactor and process engineering, industrial integration, digitalisation, system optimisation, life cycle assessment, and techno-economic analysis. Complementarity and Value Chain Coverage: We cover the full CCU value chain, from CO2 generation and capture to conversion, validation, and end-use. Industrial CO2 streams are provided by TOR, enabling real operating conditions from the outset. Capture, purification, and system integration are addressed by TRSA, supported by material development and catalytic conversion from UNIZAR and ACC. Process validation and industrial compatibility are ensured through ORL and TOR, covering both petrochemical and ceramic applications. System optimisation, monitoring, and scale-up are supported by IRIS through Digital Twin and data-driven approaches, while INEGI provides techno-economic, environmental, and sustainability assessment. ADM brings expertise in the implementation of the EU Innovation Fund’s GHG Avoidance Methodology. Exploitation and market alignment are driven by ICP, ADM, and BOP, ensuring that technical results translate into deployable solutions, along with standardisation activities (UNE). Industrial and Exploitation Dimension: We include strong industrial participation, ensuring a credible pathway towards deployment and commercialisation. TRSA provides engineering and integration capabilities for large-scale industrial systems, while TOR acts as the industrial host, providing the CO2 source, hosting the pilot implementation, and enabling validation of both use cases under real operating conditions. ORL ensures alignment with real market needs in petrochemicals. IRIS strengthens the industrial dimension through advanced monitoring, Digital Twin, and data-driven optimisation capabilities, directly supporting scalable and competitive operation. ACC contributes to circular economy integration through battery recycling value chains, enabling CO2 utilisation in secondary raw material recovery. INEGI supports industrial decision-making through techno-economic and environmental validation, while ICP and BOP ensure that exploitation pathways, IP management, and market positioning (ADM) and standardisation (UNE) are addressed from early stages. This combined industrial, technological, and commercial capacity ensures a clear pathway to scale-up, industrial uptake, and replication across European industrial clusters. Roles and Contributions: As summarised in Table 17, each partner has a clearly defined role aligned with our expertise and resources, as summarised below, and whereby all partners occupy roles consistent with their capabilities and have access to the infrastructure required to deliver our contributions. TRSA is also a recognised technology developer and licenser in metal extraction, decarbonisation, and energy transition processes and equipment. We will act as Project Coordinator leading WP11-13. We have the project management, industrial, and engineering expertise and credibility to lead this ambitious project to success. ACC, founded in 1995, is a technology-based treatment company to manage secondary raw materials. We specialise in sustainable battery recycling for Li-ion and nickel-based batteries. In CO2HESION, we will optimise and validate several routes for the utilisation of the captured CO2 in EoL battery recycling processes to recover lithium carbonate from secondary sources. TOR is headquartered in Castellón, Spain, and is a globalised multinational family business with subsidiaries in 29 countries that is dedicated to supplying products, services, solutions, and future trends to the ceramics and glass sectors (frits, glazes, pigments, inks). They also have wide expertise in the use and modelling of fluidised bed reactors. UNIZAR will develop a fluidisable tandem catalyst for CO2 hydrogenation to olefins, by combining in each particle catalytic materials for CO2 hydrogenation to olefins and catalyst for methanol-to-olefins. They will characterise the material and its performance in CO2 hydrogenation to olefins and will obtain a kinetic model suitable for reactor modelling. They will study the fluid-dynamic behaviour of the developed materials (attrition, segregation). They will test the catalyst/sorbent in a sorption enhanced reactor with continuous sorbent flow. 3.2 Capacity of participants and consortium as a whole: Our consortium is made up of 16 partners from 8 EU countries, and brings the necessary expertise and disciplines in polymer chemistry, composite engineering, industrial manufacturing, sensing and actuation, D T and AI systems, durability testing, etc. for the engineering of a new class of vitrimer composites that enable reprocessing, self-healing, and reversible assembly, while embedding cost-effective resistive sensors and thermal actuators directly into the composite architecture for product monitoring, smart maintenance and repair strategies in support of a circular, low-carbon, and digital construction sector. We have a strong materials and chemistry cluster (PCCL, FTPO, HELIOS, BME), which provides the scientific and industrial basis for our vitrimer platform development. We also have a manufacturing and structural composites cluster, made up of TUL, OST-IWK, and Fiber Elements (FE) that will ensure that our project’s material and sensor innovations and their embedding into manufacturable, structurally relevant components and assemblies. The digital intelligence and modelling cluster (IRIS, TUD) is responsible for the development of a robust and traceable decision-support system tailored to the behaviour of vitrimer composite structures. Our validation and demonstration partners (HASK, FE, FIBRECORE EUROPE, HOL) strengthen our consortium, as do partners dedicated to circularity, sustainability, safety, standardisation, market and exploitation, and internationalisation strategies (AITIP, UNE, HOL, UP, NLA). There is significant industrial involvement in our project, with the participation of leading industrial and technology partners across the full value chain, from material development and processing to system integration and deployment. The industrial actors (HELIOS, HASK, FIBRECORE) provide direct manufacturing capability, pilot-scale validation environments, and clear pathways to market uptake, while technology-oriented partners (IRIS, FE, FIBRECORE) ensure the integration of advanced sensing, digital intelligence, and process optimisation into industrial workflows. This involvement will ensure technology scale-up, industry adoption, and commercial pathways to market.
